data.title: Zoetis: Undervalued And Oversold
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Occasionally, I enjoy watching an episode or two of Shark Tank. Not necessarily because I believe in the direct success of the companies that show up there or because I enjoy the billionaire investors who appear there. What I enjoy is seeing unexpectedly successful entrepreneurial efforts, especially when entrepreneurs tap into a sector of the market that is typically overlooked or not thought of. For example, one company that showed up on Shark Tank was a company that offered you a DNA test for your cat. Unsurprisingly, many of the billionaires thought that this was an entirely foolish product. When you can go to the local pound and get a cat for $50, why would you pay $100 for your cat's DNA test?

They underestimated the love cat owners have for their feline companions. Basepaws, the company pitched, enjoyed the investment from one "shark" and eventually was bought out by a major healthcare provider for animals. Who bought them? The company we are going to focus on today.

Let's dive in!

A Beaten-Down Animal Health Play

Zoetis (ZTS) is the largest animal health company on the planet. This is a well-established business with over 70 years of operating history. Zoetis is also poised to generate $9.4 billion in total revenue for 2025.

Not surprisingly, the company is remarkably well-balanced. It has around 300 product lines in seven major product categories. Its blockbuster products include the dog parasiticide Simparica Trio, the dermatology dog medicine dubbed Apoquel, the osteoarthritis pain treatment for dogs called Librela, and the cat allergy relief medication known as Solensia.

Zoetis is especially well-positioned to capitalize on the secular trend of the humanization of pets. That's because about two-thirds of the company's mix comes from companion animal products, while the remaining one-third is from livestock products. A survey from Pew Research from a few years ago found that 62% of Americans owned at least one pet. Almost all pet owners (97%) thought pets were a part of their family. Over half (51%) of pet owners considered pets to be family members on the level of human members.

As a result, the global animal health market is expected to grow from around $50 billion in 2025 to $85 billion to $95 billion by 2035. This would be equivalent to a 5% to 6% CAGR and implies Zoetis controls nearly 20% of the market.

The company's commitment to research and development has analysts confident in its long-term growth capacity. The FAST Graphs analyst consensus for annual adjusted diluted EPS growth ranges from the high single digits to the low double digits for 2026 through 2028. That's off an anticipated 2025 base of $6.34.

Zoetis is financially sturdy, too. The company enjoys a BBB+ S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

Zoetis comes with a market-beating 1.6% dividend yield as well. The payout is secure, with the payout ratio likely to be in the low-30% range for 2026. The Quant System's forward annual dividend per share growth rate of 12.8% is a bit optimistic in my opinion. Still, I think Zoetis should have no problem putting up at least 9% or 10% dividend growth over the next several years. That would be a Chowder number of nearly 11 to 12.

At the current $125 share price, shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 19 (98% weight for 2026 and 2% weight for 2027). That's far below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 31.8, per FAST Graphs.

In the years to come, I believe that a realistic fair value P/E ratio for Zoetis is around 25. This is because its 8.9% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is moderately less than its 10-year average CAGR of 14.2%. Such a valuation considers the company's lower growth prospects and implies the stock is priced 24% below my fair value ($170 fair value per share estimate).

If Zoetis reverts to fair value and meets growth projections, it could post a 33% total return by the end of 2026. Through 2030, Zoetis could generate nearly 15% annual total returns.

Notable risks to the company include the diminishing safety profile of its monoclonal antibody therapies for osteoarthritis in dogs (Librela) and cats (Solensia), and industry competition.

Data from May 2023 through March 2025 reported over 12,000 instances of adverse drug events in dogs for Librela. Another 6,000-plus instances of adverse drug events were reported for cats for Solensia. It's worth noting that these are reported instances and not confirmed causal links. While Zoetis maintains full confidence in these products, the risk is that litigation could potentially erode vet trust, regardless of whether they were causal.

The other risk is that in a market as large as animal health, there's bound to be plenty of competition. If Zoetis can't keep launching innovative products, it risks losing market share to competitors. That could hurt its growth profile.

Conclusion

Zoetis is heavily undervalued and a highly attractive avenue to enjoy dividends and growth from a unique aspect in many of our lives. We own and love our animal companions and spend hundreds annually to take care of them. Pets become beloved members of our families. You can make their care a beloved part of your portfolio as well.

This month, my wife found and rescued a 6-week-old kitten from under a local Goodwill's shipping container. We took it to the vet for a check-up, and it has readily moved into our home. Guess what I decided to order to satisfy my curiosity? A Basepaws DNA test from Amazon (AMZN). Why? I wanted to know more about this kitten's breed and potential future health issues.

My goal from my dividend-paying portfolio is to enjoy gains and dividends from all aspects of life around me. The more diligently I search for high-quality, discounted opportunities, the more avenues I uncover to achieve my goal. I picture my portfolio as a fortress on a hill, protecting and providing for my family's financial future and present. Your portfolio can do just that, too. We can be Dividend Kings together.

data.title: Why REITs Beat Rental Properties In 2026
data.description:
Real estate, while more boring than tech stocks or crypto, has historically delivered some of the best returns of any asset class over the long run:

Not only that, but it has generated these strong returns all while also paying significant income, protecting against inflation, and providing valuable diversification benefits.

Therefore, most investors understand that they should include a real estate component as part of a well-diversified portfolio.

The more difficult question to answer is how to invest in real estate.

Should you buy a rental property, or simply invest in publicly listed REITs (VNQ)?

I have been doing both for a decade plus, and strongly believe that today, especially, you should favor REITs as we go into 2026.

Here are 3 reasons why:

Large Discounts to NAV

During most times, REITs tend to trade at a small premium to their net asset values (NAV), and this makes sense given that you are getting real estate exposure with the added benefits of liquidity, diversification, and professional management.

However, there are exceptional times when REITs fall out of favor and trade at unusually low valuations relative to the value of the real estate they own.

We find ourselves in such a situation today. After suffering a 3-year long bear market, REITs are now priced at near their lowest valuations ever:

There are plenty of REITs trading at 30-50% discounts relative to the real estate they own, net of debt.

Here are a few examples:

  • BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA) is the owner of apartment communities in rapidly growing markets of the Texas Triangle. It trades today at a 27% discount to our estimate of its net asset value.
  • Kimco Realty (KIM) owns grocery-anchored strip centers that are enjoying steady rent growth, has an A-rated balance sheet, and trades at an estimated 33% discount to NAV.
  • Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH) owns trophy mixed-use properties in growing Sunbelt markets, but trades at a 50% discount right now.

What this essentially means is that investors have the opportunity to invest in real estate at a steep discount via the REIT market and get the added benefits of liquidity, diversification, etc., for free on top of that.

Historically, whenever REITs have traded at such steep discounts, they have generated strong returns over the next few years and massively outperformed the returns of private real estate.

This makes sense because the discounted valuation results in higher cash flow yields. To return to the earlier examples, Armada Hoffler is currently trading at 7x its cash flow, which essentially means that it is offering a 14% cash flow yield. It pays out 8% in dividends and retains the rest to reinvest in growth.

Then, on top of that, the REIT offers up to 100% upside potential as it eventually reprices at closer to its net asset value. This may not happen overnight, but historically, REITs always eventually recover from bear markets and reprice at near their NAVs:

Put simply, the discounted valuation results in a higher yield and greater long-term upside potential, which then results in higher total returns.

Lower Financing Cost

Another important reason to favor REITs is that they give you access to cheaper debt.

Today, if you wanted to buy a private property, you would have a hard time financing it, as mortgage rates are often higher than property cap rates.

The average 30-year mortgage currently has a 6.2% interest rate, but most high-quality apartment communities sell at cap rates in the low to mid 5s, resulting in a negative financing spread. It then forces private buyers to invest in properties without mortgages, which may reduce long-term return.

REITs are different.

Most do them locked in long-term mortgages at much lower interest rates before they surged. As a result, investors can still get the benefits of these lower rates by investing in REITs today.

You are essentially buying equity into a real estate portfolio at a discount, and then getting the added benefits of pre-arranged financing at below-market rates. Normally, this would be worth a premium in the private markets, but you get it at a discount through the REIT market.

To give you an example: NNN REIT (NNN) is one of the leading net lease REITs, and its BBB+ rated balance sheet and smart timing by its management allowed it to secure long-term debt at a 4.3% interest rate, and its average remaining debt maturity is 12 years.

You could not get anything remotely close to this if you were to buy a rental property today.

Greater Risk-and-Hassle-Adjusted Returns

Studies show that REITs are more rewarding investments than private real estate over the long run, and this makes sense when you consider all the advantages that they enjoy:

  • Economies of scale
  • Better access to capital
  • Development profits
  • Additional service profits
  • Best talent working for you
  • Superior specialty assets like data centers, cell towers, e-commerce warehouses, etc.
  • And many other reasons...

We also saw earlier that the outperformance grows even larger in the periods following low REIT valuations:

That on its own is enough of a reason for most investors to favor REITs: they are expected to be more rewarding over the long run.

But it does not end here.

REITs are also safer and require less effort than private real estate investments.

They are safer because they are public, liquid companies that are professionally-managed, widely diversified, use conservative levels of leverage, own Class A assets, and protect shareholders from potential liability issues.

Private real estate is the opposite. They are private, illiquid investments that are highly concentrated, typically use high leverage, and face significant liability risk.

Beyond that, REITs provide true passive income. You will never get calls from tenants, contractors, or anyone else. You can just sit back and enjoy the returns.

I would much rather earn a 10% return with lower risk and zero effort than chase a potential 12% return with constant worry and work. But in the case of REITs, you still get a higher expected return despite taking less risk and requiring less effort.

Closing Note

I invest in both REITs and private real estate. There are better and worse times for both. As an example, back in 2021, REITs were coming off a strong rally, finding new opportunities was getting harder, and this led me to buy a private property and invest in private real estate-backed loans.

But today, the situation is the reverse. Private markets are fairly expensive, even as REITs are heavily discounted. For this reason, we strongly favor REIT investments at High Yield Landlord at the moment.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: The Market's Overreaction Is The Long-Term Investor's Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, Dividend King Abbott Laboratories (ABT) saw its price drop due to a mixed Q4 earnings report. While the company continues to display resilience, headwinds in some of their segments continue to have a negative impact.

Notably, their Nutrition segment experienced a decline, which triggered the steep sell-off in price.

While I view this as an overreaction from the market, long-term investors should ignore the noise and consider buying the dip.

In this article, I break down Abbott's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why long-term investors should take advantage of the sell-off.

Previous Thesis

I covered Abbott Laboratories back in December, initiating a buy rating on the stock. Although they appeared somewhat expensive, their operational resilience and 7% dividend hike amidst uncertainty were reasons I was bullish.

Their pending acquisition of Exact Sciences, anticipated to close in Q2'26 is also expected to drive growth and be accretive to earnings.

However, despite my buy rating, I did warn investors that any significant upside would likely be limited. And that they should consider adding on any signs of share price weakness, preferably near $100 a share.

Since, the stock is down more than 12% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), also down slightly over the same time frame.

Why The Dip?

As mentioned previously, Abbott Laboratories saw their share price slide after reporting Q4 earnings. ABT slid 5% in pre-market trading as their Nutritional segment weighed on company earnings.

This is basically adding insult to injury, as their prior quarter saw management narrow guidance. EPS guidance was narrowed to $5.12 - $5.18, down from $5.10 - $5.20 prior.

While the company also missed revenue estimates in Q3, Q4 saw an even bigger miss. Revenue amounted to $11.46 billion. While this did manage to increase from $11.37 billion in Q3, ABT missed analysts' estimates by a sizable $340 million.

In the third quarter, they only missed $20 million. So, this could have also spooked investors.

Revenue managed to see slight growth of 4.4% from the year prior, while organic sales growth amounted to 3%. For the entire year, sales totaled $44.3 billion, up 6.7% from 2024.

Abbott's performance was impacted by a 8.9% decline in its Nutrition segment. Sales declined from $2.13 billion to $1.94 billion, driven by continued market share loss. Partly, this was because of the loss of a large contract.

Diagnostics saw a lesser decline of 2.5% as sales fell from $2.52 billion to $2.46 billion.

Both overshadowed the performance in the Medical Devices segment that saw sales increase 12.3% year-over-year.

Still, despite this, the Dividend King managed to achieve double-digit bottom line growth of 10.27%.

Gross margins increased 200 basis points to 57.1%. Operating margins also increased 150 basis points to 25.8%. And this was despite higher inflation and tariffs. So, ABT's performance overall was solid.

2026 Outlook

Management stated they expect growth in their Nutrition segment to pick up in the latter half of the year, driven by product innovation and execution. They also initiated guidance for EPS in a range of $5.55 to $5.80. This represents 10% growth at midpoint, solid all things considered.

Sales are projected to see growth between 6.5% - 7.5%, higher than the 4.4% they saw in the fiscal year.

While economic factors like inflation, tariffs, and headwinds in China continue to present challenges, promotion shifts, product innovation, pricing, the pending acquisition, and cost-savings initiatives are anticipated to mitigate risks.

A Safe & Reliable Dividend

As I discussed in my previous article, Abbott's 7% dividend raise amidst uncertainty was a reason to have confidence in the stock. They also have 54 years of consecutive increases with a dividend well-covered by cash flows.

Furthermore, free cash flow is projected to grow at a healthy rate, ensuring that Abbott's dividend will likely remain safe for the foreseeable future.

The company's balance sheet also gives them dividend flexibility. Their pending acquisition of Exact Sciences was purchased (all cash) for $21 billion.

So, for investors worried about how this impacts the balance sheet going forward, this is nothing investors should worry about.

With a market cap over $200 billion, robust liquidity, and total debt of roughly $13 billion, Abbott Laboratories remains in a comfortable position financially.

The Sell-Off Presents An Opportunity

Currently, Abbott Laboratories trades at $108.58 a share, a price not seen since mid-2024. Using their 2026 midpoint, this gives them a forward P/E of 19.13x. This is below their 5-year average of 24.17x, offering a potentially attractive entry point for long-term investors.

Using their normal multiple, this puts the share price around $137 a share. This is lower than Wall Street's current price target of approximately $145. While I do anticipate heightened volatility in the near term, ABT's current sell-off should be seen as a buying opportunity.

Risks & Conclusion

Going forward, investors should keep an eye out for continued headwinds in their Nutritional segment. If market share losses continue, I suspect more volatility as the price would likely fall below $100 a share.

But long-term investors should view this as a buying opportunity, as lower interest rates will likely provide tailwinds by lowering costs. Tariff headwinds will also likely dissipate in the next year as well.

If you're a long-term investor looking for a safe and reliable dividend from a company with staying power, then consider Abbott Laboratories' price dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: MercadoLibre: Incredible Growth Acceleration Amid LatAm Fears
data.description:
Without a doubt, the Trump administration's recent operations in Venezuela have stirred the global geopolitical order. At home in Latin America, many companies are now facing the possibility of more interventionist policy from the United States. And yet amid the confusion, there remain many bargain plays that offer great growth potential for investors who are looking to gain international exposure. Amid stretched valuations for U.S. equities, I remain very enthusiastic about the prospects for international stocks to keep outperforming domestic in 2026.

MercadoLibre (MELI), the largest e-commerce titan in Latin America and often referred to as the "Amazon of Brazil" is looking particularly appealing after a recent slide. Since peaking briefly above $2,600 last May, MercadoLibre has lost ~20% of its value, putting the stock in correction territory.

Geopolitical risk is, of course, ever-present, but that's true not just of MercadoLibre, but of all companies, whether in the U.S. or Europe, especially with the specter of additional tariffs looming ahead. MercadoLibre looks like a fantastic rebound buy, as its recent fundamentals have shown meaningful acceleration, despite a global macroeconomic slowdown. I'm initiating MercadoLibre at a buy rating.

All-in-one commerce platform for Latin America

First, let's get a solid grounding on MercadoLibre's scope. The marketplace giant organizes itself into five business units across two core brands: MercadoLibre, which is the company's "commerce" division, and MercadoPago, which is the fintech division.

MercadoLibre's principal business unit, Commerce, needs no introduction. This is essentially an Amazon-equivalent, prominent in Brazil, Argentina, and Mexico. The company operates a third-party marketplace that allows sellers to transact their wares, while also operating a chunk of first-party sales as well. The company also runs a suite of advertising tools to help sellers boost visibility for their products. On top of this, the company's core value-add is its broad logistics network. The company touts nearly a hundred logistics centers and delivers the majority of its orders within a 48-hour window.

The company's MercadoPago division, meanwhile, consists of the Acquiring business unit, which handles payment processing solutions for both online and offline transactions, and Fintech Services, which is a loan-generating arm. It's worth noting that MercadoLibre extends its balance sheet into multiple forms of loans, from credit cards and personal loans to business lines of credit, including those secured by assets. MercadoLibre also offers savings and checking accounts, operating as a digital bank. Needless to say, MercadoLibre is hands-down one of the super-apps and major internet conglomerates of its region. As regards its banking activities, we also find it worthwhile to note that it has a high-profile board member in Nicolas Aguzin, an Argentine national who served as CEO of JPMorgan's Latin America division (and then went on to serve as head of Asia) - which to me, gives MercadoLibre tremendous international credibility.

The right-hand side of the chart below showcases the markets in which MercadoLibre is most prominent. Brazil is by far the company's largest market, with revenue of 4.01 billion (in USD terms) representing 54% of the company's revenue in the most recent Q3. On a consolidated basis, commerce services (including logistics and advertising) comprised 56% of the company's revenue, while loans and payments made up the remainder.

Mexico is its second-largest market, comprising 22% of revenue, and Argentina (also where the company was founded) is its third-largest market, with 19% of revenue. Today, the company is headquartered in Uruguay. All other markets, which are all located within Central or South America, represent just 4% of the company's total. So, that said, unless there are country-specific tensions rising in Brazil, Argentina, or Mexico, any unrest that may be happening in one country (like Venezuela) may not be incredibly material to MercadoLibre's overall business.

Recent acceleration in order growth and users

Now with a solid grounding on the company's business lines and its core markets, let's dig a bit more into the meat of why MercadoLibre makes for an attractive investment now. Despite investors' waning risk tolerance for Latin America, MercadoLibre has shown tremendous recent growth.

The chart below showcases the trends in the company's GMV, or gross merchandise value, by market.

We note total GMV soared 28% y/y to $16.5 billion. This is in U.S. dollar terms (MercadoLibre is listed on the NASDAQ, and reports in USD); on a constant-currency basis, the company notes growth would have been even stronger at 35% y/y. Brazil and Mexico both accelerated sharply to 36% y/y growth, from the high-teens previously, which was partially offset by softness in Argentina (which was mostly due to wild currency fluctuations in the Argentine peso: on a constant currency basis, the company notes Argentina GMV would have grown 44% y/y).

Management notes that the core growth driver for its commerce portfolio was its decision to reduce free shipping thresholds in Brazil, which opened the floodgates to a raft of new buyers. As shown below, the company added a staggering 6 million net-new active buyers in the quarter, growing 26% y/y to a record of 76.8 million active transacting users:

An incredible stat is that a large ~4 million chunk of these new users are brand-new to MercadoLibre (have rarely ever transacted with the platform before), pulled in by the company's lower shipping minimums. Per CEO Ariel Szarfsztejn's remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q3 earnings call:

Out of those, more or less 4 million were new buyers to the platform. And then the others, there were buyers that did buy at some point in MercadoLibre, we believe both the total number of buyers and the number of new users are -- is very healthy, and it's a great testament of everything we are doing in MercadoLibre by improving the value proposition, both in Brazil, where we are lowering the free shipping threshold, take rates and so on, which you know, but also in the other countries, we had a great quarter in Mexico. We had a great quarter in Chile, Colombia, Argentina, which Martin was referring to.

So we are excited with everything that is happening with the vibrancy that we are generating in our platform and the engagement and frequency that we see from those users that we're bringing into the platform."

I view GMV/active buyer growth as the most critical indicator of MercadoLibre's health, as marketplace sales are the anchor product that drive growth in all the other segments. The healthy GMV trends have also led to higher TPV (total payment volumes) for the fintech arm. As shown below, payment processing TPV also grew at an accelerated 32% y/y pace to $47.4 billion:

Meanwhile, the company's other financial services businesses also continued to thrive. Assets under management, which is the cumulative value of all the savings and checking accounts that MercadoLibre has issued, grew nearly 2x y/y to $15.07 billion.

Meanwhile, the loans outstanding/credit portfolio also swelled 83% y/y to $11.02 billion. We note that the company isn't sacrificing credit quality to grow its loan business. As shown below, the ratio of loans that are 90 days past due fell -30bps to 17.6%. While this does seem high in the context of a regular bank, we note that ~80% of MercadoLibre's credit exposure is to higher-risk, higher-reward consumer loans and credit cards.

We note that MercadoLibre has hardly sacrificed profitability in order to spike user growth. Yes, free shipping did drag down COGS by -260bps y/y in Q3: however, the company offset their headwind elsewhere with overhead savings on G&A costs and product development costs. Overall adjusted operating margins stood at 9.8%, down only -70bps y/y despite the headwinds to logistics costs.

Adjusted EBITDA, meanwhile, grew 31% y/y to $933 million:

Q4 look-ahead and valuation

The next major catalyst on the horizon for MercadoLibre is its Q4 earnings print, expected in early or mid-February. These are the core items we're watching:

  • Continued growth acceleration in GMV. It's a critical holiday quarter for consumer spending, but early indications from a January retail conference showcase that at least in the U.S., consumer spending appears to still be growing. We expect MercadoLibre also to report healthy holiday sales results in its core markets and continue its recent trend of active buyer acquisition and GMV growth.
  • Continued credit portfolio improvements. MercadoLibre has also originated a burst of new loans recently. We're hopeful that as loans mature, the company continues to showcase improvements in its non-performing loan ratios.
  • Commentary on the political landscape. We also expect MercadoLibre to reassure investors on the stability in its business despite recent geopolitical headlines in Latin America, and to offer a robust outlook ahead for calendar 2026.

In spite of MercadoLibre's many growth levers, we find that the stock trades at a very attractive ~20x forward adjusted EBITDA:

Considering 30%+ top-line growth and adjusted EBITDA growth, with opportunities for margin leverage once the company comps past its free shipping offer in Brazil, we think this is a great price to pay for a compelling growth stock that is dominant in its LatAM region. We are cognizant of the fact, however, that MercadoLibre does trade at a slight premium to Sea (SE), which is a similar e-commerce conglomerate in Southeast Asia. That said, we note that MercadoLibre's e-commerce operations have richer structural profitability than Sea's Shopee division, and Sea relies more on its more volatile gaming division for a large chunk of its profitability.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, MercadoLibre isn't without its risks. The Trump administration is currently very antagonistic against the liberal regime in Brazil, which is MercadoLibre's largest market. Consumer spending also remains on tenterhooks, and there's no guarantee that Christmas sales will be healthy when MercadoLibre reports Q4 results. Macroeconomic uncertainty could also threaten credit health in the company's growing loan portfolio.

That said, we think the market has been overly attuned to MercadoLibre's risks, as the stock fell ~20% over the past half year. In my view, risks are now fully priced in: take advantage of the dip to buy.

data.title: Cathay General Bancorp: The Setup Looks Good For 2026
data.description:
I think this year could be a good one for U.S. banks. Between cheaper funding and fixed-asset repricing, the outlook for interest margins and pre-provision income growth looks decent. Couple that with resilient credit trends and improving capital ratios (unlocking more buybacks), and you have all the ingredients for a good year in terms of the industry's bottom line.

With the above in mind, it makes sense to look for banks with above-average leverage to these trends. That's why I continue to favor Los Angeles-based Cathay General Bancorp (CATY), which just reported a strong rise in its quarterly and annual earnings.

Rating it a "Buy" in my last update, I felt that Cathay's above-average deposit costs and skew to fixed-rate lending left it well-placed to capture margin-driven growth. And with the shares trading for just 11.5 times trailing earnings, I didn't think that was reflected in its valuation. With another quarter of results to analyze, now is an appropriate time to review that rating. I remain bullish, with Cathay well-placed to deliver another strong year of growth in 2026.

Margin Expansion Still Playing Out…

Picking up from last time, Cathay continues to benefit from a wider net interest margin ("NIM"), which has now expanded for six straight quarters. That run includes another 5bps of expansion since my last piece, taking Cathay to 3.36% as of Q4 2025. Alongside a growing balance sheet, this has been a boon for the bank's net interest revenue, which rose 14% year-on-year last quarter to $195 million.

The first thing to consider about margin-driven growth is that it is highly accretive to earnings. A bank doesn't need to hire more employees or open new branches just to service a higher net interest margin, whereas it may have to do these things if revenue growth was being led by volume (i.e. higher balances of interest-earning assets). As a result, it typically drives very strong operating leverage. This is exactly what Cathay has seen, with year-over-year NII growth outpacing costs by almost 600bps last quarter. That's been great for its earnings; last quarter's pre-tax profit was 30% more than a year ago.

The second point I would make is somewhat similar. If Cathay's net interest revenue growth was a result of making more loans, it would have to retain more capital to back them. That doesn't really apply to margin-driven growth, making it highly accretive to the bank's return on capital. Again, this is exactly what Cathay has seen. It booked a 14.25% return on its tangible common equity in Q4, nearly 125bps higher than the year-ago quarter.

…And That Should Continue In 2026

These trends should continue in 2026. One thing working in Cathay's favor is its funding position. The bank has a relatively high cost of interest-bearing deposits (3.12% last quarter), while only around 16% of its deposits are in non-interest-bearing accounts. This works in Cathay's favor when interest rates are falling. In terms of the numbers, management is guiding for a 60% beta on its interest-bearing deposits. Given it expects the Fed to cut by 50bps this year, that means Cathay should be able to improve on the 3.12% it was paying last quarter.

At the same time, there are some supportive trends on the asset side of the ledger. For example, 60% of Cathay's loans have a fixed rate of interest. The repricing of older vintages is allowing the bank to pick up some yield here. These will be loans made back in 2021-2022, when interest rates were much lower than they are today. While its blended loan yield, which includes its variable-rate loans, is still falling on the back of lower interest rates, it is doing so modestly. Similarly, Cathay shouldn't see much of a headwind coming out of its bond portfolio, where the blended yield was 3.24% last quarter.

Weighing all that up, Cathay is guiding for a 2026 net interest margin of 3.4%-3.5%. Taking the midpoint of that range, this would map to around 15bps of NIM expansion this year. That may not sound like much, but Cathay has around $23 billion in interest-earning assets on its balance sheet. All else equal, an additional 15bps of margin would add $35 million to its annual net interest income. And like I said, this would mostly drop straight down to its pre-tax earnings. Cathay earned $315 million in net income last year. An extra $35 million wouldn't be trivial, even after taxes.

Credit Quality & Capital Look Strong

A solid pre-provision growth outlook isn’t the only thing to like about Cathay. Credit quality trends have been solid as well. While there were bouts of concern within the regional banking space last year ("Liberation Day" tariffs, NDFI exposure, and so on), Cathay hasn’t seen a major uptick in losses. Quite the opposite, with the bank recording a net charge-off rate of just 11bps last quarter. It has also seen a fall in non-performing assets and classified loans.

Cathay's balance sheet looks in good shape as well, as it ended the year with a strong 13.3% Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio. With earnings growing and capital healthy, Cathay has been able to step up its share repurchases, with management announcing it spent $52 million on share buybacks last quarter. That took full-year buyback spending to just under $180 million, $95 million more than in 2024. As a result, Cathay’s share count fell 5% last year on an end-of-period basis. Given its earnings look set to grow nicely, there's a good chance the bank will be able to build on this in 2026.

Shares Still Look Cheap

Between growing net income and Cathay’s share repurchases, the outlook for its earnings per share growth looks strong. Indeed, the sell-side analysts that track the bank think EPS will grow 17% this year. Meanwhile, its shares have barely risen since my last update, trailing the wider regional bank space by a few points.

As a result, Cathay's stock still looks cheap. Shares trade for $50.73 as of this writing, equal to around 11.2 times last year's EPS. Given its strong growth outlook, this falls to just 9.5 times consensus 2026 earnings.

While that looks attractive in itself, it also stacks up well next to peers. The State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) is one fund that tracks the industry. According to its manager, the industry trades for 11.8 times 1-year forward earnings. If Cathay commanded that multiple, its share price would be roughly 25% higher.

That said, investors don't really need any uplift in the valuation for an investment to work. As an example, if Cathay were to trade for a modest 10.5 times 2026 EPS a year from now, then its stock would be $56 assuming it meets consensus. That would be good for a 10% return. Including its dividend, this would rise to 13%, leaving investors with a margin of a safety. For that reason, I remain bullish, and I continue to rate shares "Buy".

data.title: JFR: Expect Dividends To Get Cut As Rates Trend Lower
data.description:
Overview

Unfortunately, I believe that the golden era of floating rate investments was in the past. As interest rates trend lower, the overall appeal of these floating rate funds continues to decline. When I previously covered Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (JFR), I issued a hold rating due to the inconsistent dividend coverage. Although there hasn't been any new reporting released, I wanted to revisit the fund to discuss the potential outlook now that interest rates have declined. I believe that there can be several outcomes, but it ultimately depends on management's ability to navigate these shifts.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that JFR's share price has declined by nearly 6.8%. The fund has struggled to regain positive momentum in recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 5.5% over the same time frame. JFR now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 12.8% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of distributions going forward.

While JFR continues to trade at a discount to NAV, I believe that the discount isn't quite appealing in the current market environment. In fact, I believe that the discount should be a bit larger considering the pressure the higher interest rates are putting on debt securities. While the market anticipates that interest rates will likely decline over the course of 2026, we don't know the size of these rate adjustments. If the Fed continues to initiate smaller 25 basis point cuts, this may not be enough to relieve some pressure within the portfolio.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest portfolio overview, JFR has total managed assets of $2.19B that is spread across 431 different holdings. JFR has a listed gross expense ratio of 2.83%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes fees related to interest and other operating costs. When excluding these other costs, JFR has actual management fees of 0.76% of assets, which may be worth it considering the active management and higher yield offered to investors.

The fund's primary objective is to achieve a high level of current income through its portfolio of adjustable rate senior loans and other debt-focused investments. Under normal conditions, JFR will allocate at least 80% of its assets into adjustable-rate loans. Therefore, the health of these investments is directly linked to interest rates. A higher interest rate can translate to a higher level of interest income collected on these loans. The portfolio's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Senior Loans: 83.8%
  • Corporate Bonds: 10.1%
  • Cash Equivalents: 5.6%
  • Net Other Assets: 1.5%
  • Common Stocks: 0.9%
  • Collateralized Loan Obligation Debt: 0.1%

Expanding on this diversity, JFR also ensures that its portfolio is allocated across different sectors. This helps mitigate any sort of concentration risk to any one specific focus area. As we can see below, JFR is most heavily allocated to information technology securities at 19.6% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to consumer discretionary and industrial securities, which accounts for 15.5% and 15.4% of assets, respectively.

Lastly, the fund is most heavily allocated to securities that have a maturity date less than 4 years out. In theory, short-dated maturities can help minimize a fund's sensitivity to interest rates. However, the impact here is minimal since the fund is still primarily composed of floating rate securities. The remaining 42% of its portfolio has a maturity between 5 to 9 years, which offers the benefit of uninterrupted cash flows for a longer time frame. The downside is that these longer dated maturities will typically decline when interest rates rise, which can explain why JFR's share price has remained suppressed over the last few years.

Valuation Risks

Since JFR is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, the fund now trades at a discount to NAV of 6.99%. For reference, JFR has traded at an average discount to NAV of 7.19% over the last five year period. Therefore, JFR now trades at a less attractive valuation in relation to its historical average. Even when referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that JFR currently trades at a price to NAV level that sits at the higher end of its historical range over the last decade. Therefore, I do not believe it is a great time to accumulate.

My issue is that it appears that JFR is still priced at the higher end of its range, despite interest rates starting to fall. For instance, the Fed has initiated three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates throughout 2025. A lower interest rate can directly translate to lower interest income collected from its portfolio of loans. However, interest rates still remain in a bit of a tricky spot because despite the recent interest rate cuts, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten year range.

As a result, the fund remains vulnerable to the possibility of borrower defaults. This is a risk for the fund since a sizeable portion of its assets are allocated to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, a credit rating of below a BBB usually means that securities are more likely to be impacted by defaults. These lower rated securities can typically be borrowers that have less consistent cash flows or may be more reliant on debt financing to support operations. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 9.2%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 89.3%
  • Not Rated: 1.6%

For instance, S&P Global estimates that the rate of defaults can rise as high as 5.1% for speculative-grade borrowers in a pessimistic scenario. This assumes that interest rate cuts are delayed, there's slowing economic growth, and other unfavorable factors to the debt markets. All of these different moving parts result in JFR's share price demonstrating a sensitivity to interest rates. As we can see below, JFR's share price has struggled to get back to its pre-pandemic levels since interest rates were hiked throughout 2022 and 2023.

On a more positive note, a lower interest rate environment relieves some pressure for JFR's existing portfolio of borrowers. This is because lower rates can translate to lower interest expenses for loans, which can improve operating spreads. Additionally, a lower cost of debt environment may encourage more borrowing activity. This may cause an influx of new borrowers in the market, which may be an opportunity for management to invest in new securities. If management is able to capitalize on this shift, it can lead to higher earnings and healthier NAV growth over time.

A lower interest rate environment also leads to higher values for these debt securities, since their operating spreads are improved and the perceived risk is a lot lower. Lastly, JFR utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 36.62% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$802.2M. This aggressive use of leverage also contributes to the fund's sensitivity to interest rates and impacts JFR's own operating spread. For instance, JFR is currently paying an annualized leverage cost of 5.15% on this debt, which shrinks its spread between the cost of debt and the yield collected from its securities. The use of leverage also has the risk of amplifying losses if allocated to securities that perform poorly.

Dividends May Decline

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.085 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.8%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that JFR's payouts have been on the steady increase since 2022. This makes sense; the higher interest rates rose, the higher interest income JFR can collect on those debt securities. However, the trajectory of interest rates has shifted, but the level of distributions being paid has not. Although there aren't any updated reports available yet, we can see the fund struggling to produce positive earnings on the last annual report for 2025. For reference, here are the annual earnings:

  • 2021: $1.58 per share
  • 2022: ($0.27) per share
  • 2023: $0.61 per share
  • 2024: $1.17 per share
  • 2025: $0.56 per share

If we take the monthly distribution of $0.085 per share and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.02 per share. As we can see by the annual earnings, the fund's ability to support this level of distribution is variable over time. As interest rates trend lower, I anticipate that JFR's internal level of net investment income will decline and threaten the sustainability of payouts. This is just the nature of these floating rate funds and shouldn't necessarily be viewed as a weakness. It just becomes challenging to recommend a position in JFR in a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward over time.

Lastly, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the distributions are being supported by net investment income. Therefore, the distributions received from JFR may not offer an attractive level of tax-efficiency for investors seeking a high rate of income generation. For this reason, a position in JFR would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on JFR at this time due to the uncertainty of the fund's outlook. While I anticipate that dividends will have to be reduced if the earnings decline, there is always the possibility that lower interest rates can serve as a positive growth catalyst over time. Lower interest rates are likely to translate to lower net investment income, but a more favorable debt environment can lead to more growth opportunities for management to capitalize on. Lower interest rates may also improve the fund's ability to provide price growth over time, so I don't necessarily believe that lower rates should make investors sell out of this fund.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street closed the trading week lower, though equities rebounded from intra-week lows following a volatile stretch marked by heightened geopolitical uncertainty. Markets digested a steady stream of earnings reports, while safe-haven demand continued to support precious metals.

Geopolitical developments took center stage after President Donald Trump reiterated his interest in Greenland during remarks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. Trump said he intends to begin negotiations “immediately” to pursue control of the territory, adding to broader global uncertainty that weighed on investor sentiment.

Meanwhile, gold extended its rally, moving closer to the $5,000-an-ounce level as investors sought refuge amid rising geopolitical tensions and renewed concerns over the Federal Reserve’s independence. The precious metal gained 8.5% over the week.

On the earnings front, results continued to drive sharp stock-specific moves. Shares of Intel (INTC) plunged just over 17% on Friday following the company’s latest earnings release, contributing to the week’s heightened market volatility.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) lost -0.4%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) dipped -0.1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -0.5%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Salesforce's (CRM) Selloff Is Overdone - A Contrarian Buy Opportunity.

Kura Oncology (KURA): Safety Profile Of Komzifti Supports Upgrade.

Nebius (NBIS) Is A Buy - I Was Wrong About AI Infrastructure Demand.

Celestica (CLS) Q4 Earnings Preview - Expect Another Double Beat.

Franco-Nevada (FNV): High-Quality Compounder Worth Paying For.

Neutral On Jack In The Box (JACK) - The Ugly Duckling Stays Ugly.

Time To Consider Exit Strategy For Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD).

D.R. Horton (DHI) Is A Sell As Affordability Concerns Seen Persisting.

This Time Is Not Different, It's Time To Sell Micron Technology (MU).

Bearish On Richtech Robotics (RR) As This Stock Is A Dilution Trap.

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -0.5% to 49,099. S&P 500 -0.4% to 6,916. Nasdaq -0.1% to 23,501. Russell 2000 -0.4% to 2,667. CBOE Volatility Index +1.5% to 16.09.

S&P 500 Sectors
Consumer Staples +0.9%. Utilities -2%. Financials -2.5%. Telecom +1.1%. Healthcare +1.1%. Industrials -1.6%. Information Technology -0.8%. Materials +2.6%. Energy +3.1%. Consumer Discretionary +0.7%. Real Estate -2.5%.

World Indices
London -0.9% to 10,143. France -1.4% to 8,143. Germany -1.6% to 24,901. Japan -0.2% to 53,847. China +0.8% to 4,136. Hong Kong -0.4% to 26,750. India -2.4% to 81,538.

Commodities and Bonds
Crude Oil WTI +2.7% to $61.07/bbl. Gold +8.4% to $4,979.7/oz. Natural Gas +70% to 5.275. Ten-Year Bond Yield -0.2 bps to 4.239.

Forex and Cryptos
EUR/USD +1.97%. USD/JPY -1.52%. GBP/USD +1.96%. Bitcoin -5.6%. Litecoin -8.1%. Ethereum -10.4%. XRP -6.6%.

Top S&P 500 Gainers
Moderna (MRNA) +24%. Micron Technology (MU) +19%. Sandisk (SNDK) +16%. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +14%. Gilead Sciences (GILD) +12%.

Top S&P 500 Losers
West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) -15%. Constellation Energy (CEG) -15%. AppLovin (APP) -14%. Abbott Laboratories (ABT) -13%. McCormick & Company (MKC) -12%.

Where will the markets be headed next week? Current trends and ideas? Add your thoughts to the comments section.

data.title: Bank Margins, Earnings Set To Expand, Possibly Defy Credit Concerns
data.description:
Net interest margin expansion has driven returns higher across the banking industry in 2025. Earnings growth should continue in the coming quarters, even as credit costs continue to migrate higher.

The take

US bank earnings have continued to grow as strong margin expansion and benign credit quality supported performance. While credit costs should continue to normalize, we expect favorable results to continue, buoyed by additional margin expansion and steady loan growth. The positive fundamental landscape and a friendlier regulatory environment should spur more bank M&A activity as acquirers see deals as an attractive and faster way to gain scale — in the overall size of the company or specific markets — that they see as necessary to effectively compete.

Investors warming up as credit conditions hold for now

Many generalist investors heading into third-quarter 2025 earnings expected and looked for credit problems in banks' results. Save for a few one-offs that emerged in the period — often related to loans to nondepository financial institutions, which have driven much of the loan growth through the first nine months of 2025 — credit quality trends largely remained benign. Valuations, particularly for large banks, improved heading into 2026 as the consumer and the economy remained resilient and credit concerns eased.

We expect credit quality to normalize from current levels due to weakness in the US consumer and some stress in commercial real estate portfolios, leading to a higher level of losses. Consumer delinquencies have risen from historical lows and student loan delinquencies have jumped back near pre-pandemic levels now that the moratorium on payments is several quarters in the rearview mirror. The deterioration offers another indicator that the US consumer is stretched, marked by slower savings rates and recent weakness in the labor market.

However, bankers say that while consumer sentiment has waned, the consumer and small businesses remain resilient. Many consumers took advantage of historically low rates to refinance their mortgages before the Fed began raising rates, reducing the impact that rate hikes would have on their debt service. Consumers and other borrowers are also expected to receive more relief in the form of additional rate cuts.

Banks have seen delinquencies in their commercial and industrial and commercial real estate loan portfolios rise off historical lows, but delinquencies stabilized in the first, second and third quarters of 2025.Banks have reduced their exposure to CRE and have arguably benefited from private credit firms growing their market share and helping support the asset class.

Some skeptics argue that lenders have downplayed credit quality deterioration by modifying loans and extending maturities further out on the horizon, invoking the phrase "extend and pretend." There is evidence that extensions have occurred in the CRE market, with the number of CRE mortgages estimated to mature declining in 2025 when compared to estimates a year earlier. What remains to be seen is whether those extensions offered borrowers enough breathing room to weather the storm and if recent rate declines position them to service their debts.

We expect higher loss content in the future, but to date, few problems have occurred. In our baseline scenario, we expect provisions to dip to 19.1% of net revenue in 2025 from 19.9% through the first nine months of 2025. That ratio is expected to increase to 22.1% in 2026 as banks prepare for tougher sledding ahead.

The higher level of provisioning will serve as a modest headwind, but earnings are still expected to rise 14.6% year over year in 2025 and grow another 3.1% in 2026.

Many bankers and members of the sell-side community expect credit trends to remain stable. If that occurred, bank earnings would grow 16.2% in 2025 and another 9.8% in 2026.

Fed cuts spell funding cost relief, further margin expansion

Banks will continue to benefit from the remixing of their balance sheets as funding costs decline, while lower-yielding assets purchased or originated when rates were low, mature and are reinvested at higher yields.

Deposit costs dropped substantially late in 2024 and early in 2025 as the highest-cost deposits, often certificates of deposits (CDs) originated when rates were lower, matured and repriced at lower rates. Banks, particularly regional and community banks, increased their reliance on CDs for funding during the Fed's tightening cycle. CD pricing appears to have peaked in the second quarter of 2024 before the Fed's first rate cut in September. Since many CDs carry one-year terms, many institutions have already experienced relief from their highest-cost CDs that likely matured in the first half of 2025.

The industry failed to record the same level of declines in the second and third quarters of 2025 but has reduced its reliance on CDs for funding. Rates cut by the Federal Reserve late in 2025 were expected to offer greater funding cost relief. Fourth-quarter results from JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) and Bank of America Corp. (BAC) offered support for that thesis, with the companies' cost of interest-bearing deposits dropping 17 basis points and 21 basis points, respectively, from the prior quarter.

Banks will need to remain competitive with alternatives in the Treasury and money markets as they seek to cut costs further. The difference between the average fed funds rate and the industry's cost of deposits declined modestly in the third quarter of 2025. That gap should narrow as additional rate cuts by the Fed allow banks to lower their deposit costs while staying competitive with higher-yielding alternatives to maintain deposit growth.

However, stronger loan growth means that banks funding needs are greater, tempering just how quickly they can lower their deposit costs. We expect banks' deposit costs to decline notably in the fourth quarter of 2025 and the first quarter of 2026, but additional funding relief will be harder to come by without further rate cuts by the Fed. The central bank is expected to cut rates again later in 2026, resulting in sharper declines in deposit costs.

Lower rates will also lead to pressure on loan yields, but this pressure will be mitigated by the replacement of historical, lower-rate assets with newer, higher-yielding alternatives. That dynamic will allow for continued margin expansion.

Looking ahead

The desire to grow earnings, gain scale and a friendlier regulatory environment should support stronger bank M&A activity. Stronger banks likely will use the positive fundamental environment to invest in technology and their franchises and remain relevant, while weaker institutions could find it more difficult to compete and could look to partner with their larger counterparts.

Bank M&A activity closed 2025 with a bang after several years of depressed activity. Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) opened the fourth quarter with plans to acquire Comerica Inc. (CMA), marking just the fourth bank deal over $10 billion in value since the financial crisis. Other sizable transactions followed, including Huntington Bancshares Inc.'s (HBAN) plans to purchase Cadence Bank (CADE) for $7.59 billion and Nicolet Bankshares Inc.'s (NIC) $866 million pending purchase of MidWestOne Financial Group Inc. (MOFG). The fourth quarter ended with eight bank deals with values over $500 million announced in the period — the most in any quarter since the first quarter of 2021.

Large buyers like Huntington and active acquirers like Nicolet have returned as regulators have signaled that they are far more supportive of M&A activity. With more buyers, the desire to gain scale to invest in technology, succession issues, pent-up demand for deals during the pandemic, and the rate hike cycle, as well as stable fundamental trends, all should lead to greater bank M&A activity.

The expected pickup in deal activity among regional and community banks would alter the competitive landscape. Buyers that gain scale through deals likely would be less focused on organic growth while integrating their acquisitions. They will also be more likely to address any concerns they have in their loan portfolios since bank M&A accounting requires the buyer to mark a target's balance sheet to market. The fair value mark would, in theory, allow an institution to purge unwarranted assets without taking large additional losses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alaska Air Is A Value Stock Worth Buying
data.description:
As you can see in the chart below, the past 52 weeks haven't been particularly kind to Alaska Air Group, Inc. (ALK). The stock has seen declines of 28% even after the December rally, and so it seems that the business is far from its best. Alaska Air reported their 2025 Q4 earnings just yesterday (Jan 22nd) and today I believe it is an opportune time to initiate coverage on the stock by looking at their results, strategy, and valuation.

In the below discussion it is shown that Q4 was generally a respectable quarter for Alaska Air. While liquidity and debt load are worth monitoring, changes for unit revenue and cost were positive. In addition to a good long-term strategy, the financial outlook for FY2026 is solid, and so I view Alaska Air stock as attractive given that the valuation is currently quite low. Therefore, I'm initiating a buy rating.

Unit Revenue Growth Outperforms

First things first, Alaska Air reported total revenues of $3.63 billion for 2025 Q4 and that missed expectations by $10.3 million. Despite the underperformance versus consensus analyst expectations, this does represent YoY growth of 3%. Note that in Q3, there was 23% of growth, but nonetheless, Q4 seemed to be a resilient quarter for the top line.

For a better measure of how efficiently Alaska Air is generating their revenues, let's take a look at the change in their RASM. As you can see in the chart above, they saw a 0.6% increase YoY. This represents a slowdown from the previous quarter, and they also missed their own expectations of ~1% growth for the metric. While this may be a bit unfavorable, note that their RASM percentage change has outperformed the Big 4 by a notable margin in the past four quarters. Even though there are signs that the Big 4 is now closing the growth gap, the ability for Alaska Air to grow RASM in 3 of the past 4 quarters is a demonstration that their revenue-generating efficiency is heading in the right direction overall. Note that if it wasn't for the U.S. Government shutdown in the quarter, results here could have been even more robust.

Unit Cost Control Has Improved

In terms of CASM percentage change when excluding fuel effects, there were generally positive developments. While the 1.3% increase in CASMex outpaced the RASM growth rate to show some shaky profitability, there has been notable improvement. Firstly, Alaska Air projected a ~3% increase for Q4 and so results show that there was better cost control than expected. Secondly, note the large drop in CASMex growth from the previous quarter. It should also be stated that 1.3% is the smallest percentage increase for unit cost in the past four quarters. That is particularly impressive considering that they were able to still grow capacity by 2.2%. Therefore, while cost control can definitely still be better, they also seem to be heading in the right direction here.

EPS Drop Less Concerning Than It Seems

For Q4, Alaska Air reported an adjusted EPS of $0.43. That is a 56% decline YoY, but this could be less concerning than it actually is. If you go and take a look at their income statement, you can see that there was a non-operating expense of $45 million versus $6 million of non-operating income in the prior year period. Therefore, the actual operating profitability of the company seems to be better than implied by the bottom line figure. Furthermore, the company was still able to beat analyst expectations by $0.32. That happened while revenues missed, so there shouldn't be too many complaints with regards to profitability.

Capital Developments

In terms of the company's financial position, there are some deteriorations that investors should be aware of. At the end of 2025, total current assets totaled $3.266 billion, and that represents a notable decline from the $3.76 billion balance at the end of 2024. Meanwhile, over the same period, current liabilities have increased from $6.145 billion to $6.589 billion, and so there has been a meaningful change in the company's liquidity. Essentially, Alaska Air's current ratio has dropped from 0.61 to 0.50. While it is normal for airlines to have lower current ratios, this deterioration is a bit eyebrow raising and investors should monitor these developments moving forward.

In the above charts, firstly, you can see that adjusted net debt to EBITDAR is a bit higher than desired. They have a target of below 1.5, and as you can see Q4 is not really a move in the right direction in that regard. The company seems a bit leveraged and that could add a bit to the investment risk. Another interesting development is the continued share repurchases. As you can see, the diluted share count has shrunk, with the company having bought back ~$30 million in stock in Q4. Why is this interesting? Well, while the company did have $185 million of operating cash flows in the quarter, they also spent $625 million on property and equipment, and so FCFs were negative. Therefore, the company is cutting into their cash pile in order to fund these buybacks. Perhaps, management believes their stock is significantly undervalued at this stage.

Strategy Looks Good

Before discussing their guidance, I want to talk a bit about their strategy. Back in late October 2025, the company announced that they will be adding originating airports on the west coast and will also add new routes to Hawaii. I believe this is a clear statement that the company is focused on expansion, and for investors, that is likely a promising direction.

As another sign that they are focused on expansion, it was reported earlier this month that Alaska Air will buy 105 Boeing (BA) 737-10s and 5 Boeing 787s. As Rob Williams of Seeking Alpha writes, this marks "the largest aircraft order in the carrier’s history and extending its delivery pipeline through 2035." In my view, this is another demonstration that Alaska Air is focused on growth.

In addition to pushing growth, it also seems that they want to improve the experience for customers. On Jan 5th, it was announced that Hawaiian Airlines would invest over $600 million over five years. As Clark Schultz of Seeking Alpha writes

The carrier plans to modernize airport spaces, upgrade technology, and retrofit aircraft interiors while also expanding community and sustainability work across Hawaii.

In my view, these are very worthwhile investments. At the end of the day, the customer experience should be one of the key areas of focus for all companies, and so I view these proposed improvements as a potential driver of long term success for the company.

Financial Outlook

Alaska Air provided guidance for both full year 2026 and for Q1 specifically. I'll focus on the annual outlook for the purposes of this discussion, as it should provide a better long term view. For capacity growth, they are expecting it to be between 2% and 3%. For the full year 2025, they saw capacity growth of 1.9%, and so there are signs that the company's expansion will accelerate a bit this year.

For adjusted EPS, they provided a rather broad range, but nonetheless, at the midpoint, they are implying growth of 105% YoY. That sure beats the 50% decline seen in 2025 from 2024 levels, and so overall profitability is expected to jump back up.

As for CAPEX, they are expecting around $1.45 billion. In 2025, they spent $1.588 billion on property and equipment, so spending also isn't a concern moving forward. As a whole, I view full year 2026 guidance as solid, and it indicates the worst is over for the company.

Value Stock

At current levels, I would say that Alaska Air is likely well within value stock territory. In the above chart, you can see that the forward P/E ratio is now back below 10 and given the solid financial outlook and growth strategy that the company has, I would say that there is considerable undervaluation right now. If you go and take a look at Seeking Alpha's valuation page for the stock, you can see that Alaska Air is currently trading substantially below its own 5 year average based on many different valuation multiples. Given the seemingly improving fortunes of the company, I would say that the risk/reward is currently in a good position for the bulls, as stock outperformance is a very real possibility moving forward.

Recap

Alaska Air clearly hasn't had an easy past year, but their Q4 results show that the company has been resilient and is performing in a decent manner, all things considered. As discussed earlier, liquidity and debt load could be improved moving forward but since the strategy and near term financial outlook are robust, I view their prospects favorably. With that in mind, I would say the value stock valuation provides a significant amount of opportunity, and so I'm initiating Alaska Air stock at a buy rating.

data.title: Record Amount Of Utility Rate Requests In 2025 Amid Affordability Concerns
data.description:
US investor-owned energy utilities requested rate increases from state regulators totaling $22.06 billion in 2025, as tracked by Regulatory Research Associates. This is the highest amount of nominal requests since RRA started tracking rate case proceedings in the early 1980s. Rate requests pulled back slightly in 2024 to $16.39 billion after three years of rising requests in 2021, 2022 and 2023.

The take

  • Another year of record utility rate requests was established in 2025. After filing record-setting rate requests in 2021, 2022 and 2023, utilities moderated their proposals in 2024, with requests totaling $16.39 billion. But in 2025, that total jumped to $22.06 billion, the highest annual total of electric and gas rate increase requests since RRA began tracking rate cases.
  • Rate requests in 2025 were again dominated by electric utilities, which requested $18.23 billion in aggregate rate increases, while gas utilities requested $3.83 billion. The electric rate filings reflect the utilities' significant capital expenditure plans for upgrading transmission and distribution (T&D) systems and installing new renewable generation and technologies to accommodate the clean energy transition, as well as fossil and nuclear generation, to support large loads. The gas filings continue to reflect investment to replace aging infrastructure. Inflation and the rising cost of capital continue to be highlighted as factors in recent case filings.
  • The growing prevalence of multiyear rate plans is a large driver of the aggregate increase. Some of the largest rate requests in the period were attributable to multiyear plans from Florida Power & Light Co. (FPL) and Consolidated Edison Co. of New York Inc. (CE)(ED), which seek to invest in sustainability, safety and reliability, support growth and storm-hardening efforts.
  • Historically, rate case filings have been more numerous in the late spring and early summer months, while more rate case decisions are usually rendered later in the year.

These general rate cases and limited-issue rider proceedings have been driven by the need to invest in infrastructure to upgrade aging T&D systems; build new gas, solar and wind generation; and implement new technologies, including smart meters, smart grid systems, cybersecurity measures, infrastructure to support electric vehicles and battery storage. The rate requests reflect the move to shutter aging fossil fuel plants amid a nationwide push toward clean energy and protect customers against growing climate-related risks such as wildfires and damaging storms. Most recently, growing needs for investment in T&D and generation to support large load customers, including data centers, have been behind some requests.

Rate requests in 2025 amounted to the highest annual total ever tracked by RRA. The busiest time for new rate case filings tends to be late spring or early summer, with the least activity observed in the fall. By contrast, the fourth quarter is generally the busiest time of the year for rate case decisions, particularly in December.

The 2025 total was 35% above the total requests in 2024 despite a 13% drop in total case filings in 2025. Rate increases requested by utilities in 2025 were 413%higher than the amount sought in 2020 at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic, when many utilities withdrew or postponed rate cases to reduce the economic impact on struggling businesses and residents.

Rising inflation, particularly for labor and costs related to the supply chain, has been a constant topic of discussion in ongoing rate cases. For example, FPL, which filed the largest rate ask in 2025, cited higher operations and maintenance expenses tied to inflation and customer growth among the key factors driving revenue requirements in 2026. FPL indicated that costs incurred in support of the capital investments have been impacted by historic inflation and the unexpected significant migration to Florida from around the country, underscoring the importance of maintaining financial strength and flexibility 2026–29.

There are regulatory constructs in certain states that may protect investor-owned utilities from rising costs. For more information, refer to State regulatory constructs offer insight on inflation threat to utility income.

Continuing wildfire mitigation efforts and storm hardening investments are driving rate case activity, particularly in the West. Public Service Co. of Colorado (PSCO) filed an application Nov. 21, 2025, seeking a $526 million rate increase. PSCO states the increase request is primarily driven by $2.068 billion of electric distribution plant additions and associated costs since its last rate case; technology and nonlabor wildfire mitigation operations and maintenance expense; and recovery of seven deferrals totaling $283.3 million. The deferrals are related to advanced grid initiatives, excess liability insurance, revenue decoupling, the 2020 wildfire mitigation plan, EV charging infrastructure, innovative clean technology projects and coal combustion residuals.

In addition to FPL and CE, some of the largest rate requests were made by Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (PCG), Duke Energy Carolinas LLC (DUK), Virginia Electric and Power Co., Duke Energy Progress LLC and Oncor Electric Delivery Co. LLC.

Number of cases

There were 135 new electric and gas rate cases, including limited-issue rider proceedings, filed in 2025, down from 156 initiated in both 2024 and 2023.

With high inflation and higher interest rates, rate case decisions remained elevated in 2025 with about 151 cases decided, down slightly from 155 cases decided in 2024, according to RRA data.

Available initial data highlights a slight slowdown in rate case activity in 2026. As of Dec. 16, 2025, RRA-covered companies have announced an additional 25 planned rate case filings through 2026. By comparison, companies had announced the filing of 30 new rate cases as of the same date in 2024. For more information, refer to Inflation, capital spending drive strong US rate case activity.

Affordability concerns persist

As we enter 2026, affordability remains a key issue shaping the US power and utility sector, moving from a background concern to a binding constraint on what utilities can earn and build. This shift is driven by mounting pressure at state and federal levels, with energy prices having risen much faster than inflation and customer dissatisfaction at historic highs. Utilities are navigating multibillion-dollar capex programs, which risk further upward pressure on bills unless offset by new strategies. The upcoming election cycle and federal policy discussions are amplifying the focus on affordability, with industry groups and utility executives highlighting the need to moderate rate growth and maintain customer trust. RRA is watching closely how affordability concerns could reshape regulatory and market dynamics.

In response, regulatory styles are evolving, with commissions increasingly adopting frameworks that force utilities to balance investment and operational efficiency within strict limits. The sector's reliance on data centers as growth drivers is becoming more pronounced, with these customers now bearing a larger share of costs, a trend that utilities must communicate transparently to regulators and the public. States with politically exposed commissions are tightening scrutiny on utility spending and bill impacts, while federal agencies like the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission and Department of Energy are expected to play a more active role in transmission planning and reliability funding.

Cognizant of rising affordability concerns, utilities have been taking steps to highlight for commissions their efforts to reduce costs and improve efficiency even as they ask for higher rates. In a rate case application filed Nov. 25, 2025, South Jersey Gas Co. (SJG) indicated it conducted an affordability analysis, which it says concludes that its prices for gas services are currently affordable for most residential customers and will remain affordable if the proposed rate change is approved. SJG included $4.3 million in annual savings and other cost reductions in its revenue request and uses competitive bidding and cost tracking to ensure capex are reasonable.

The surge in requested rate increases comes amid rising capex by the energy utility sector. 2025 is anticipated to be another record year for utility capital investments, with the aggregate forecast capex of $214.293 billion for the 47 tracked energy utilities when results are tallied, according to an RRA analysis. This figure would exceed the $173 billion spent in 2024 by about 23%.

Spending is driven by pent-up demand to replace and modernize aging infrastructure, as well as renewable portfolio standards of multiple states that call for large expansions in low-carbon energy generation capacity. Additionally, federal infrastructure investment plans aim to convert the nation's power generation network to zero-carbon sources while investing in new generation to support strong electric growth. For the most recent report on capex, refer to Energy utility capex forecast nears $1.2 trillion in 2025–29.

Data centers — which underpin AI, digital services and cloud infrastructure — are rapidly becoming a significant new driver of power demand in various regions. This trend is expected to result in substantial capital investments for years to come, including massive investment in natural gas generation, new nuclear capacity and reviving retired coal plants or those planned for retirement. In the US, demand from data centers is projected to increase at double-digit rates through the end of the decade, fundamentally altering the overall demand landscape and placing additional strain on utility electric grids.

Multiyear rate change proposals

Legislation passed at the state level over the past several years has driven an increase in multiyear rate change requests, whereby utilities can request incremental rate increases over several years. RRA considers these plans to be a constructive ratemaking approach. In 2025, at least 16 rate cases that were filed requested multiyear rate changes. That is down from 25 such cases filed in 2024 and 21 multiyear rate case filings in 2023, which was equal to the number of multiyear requests filed in both 2021 and 2022.

Just as in 2024 and 2023, some of the largest rate cases filed in 2025 were multiyear requests. In aggregate, FPL initiated a large rate case (Docket 20250011-EI) with the Florida Public Service Commission, seeking a multiyear increase in electric base rates totaling $2.472 billion between 2026 and 2027. The rate increase is intended to fund investments to support system growth and reliability, diversify the company's power generation fleet, add solar generation and energy storage capacity, and improve resilience and security at critical facilities. On Nov. 20, 2025, the PSC voted to approve a settlement agreement authorizing FPL a $1.65 billion rate increase in 2026 and 2027, or two-thirds of FPL's ask.

Long-term perspective

From a historical perspective, the nominal $22.04 billion requested by utilities in 2025 significantly exceeded rate requests in the early 1980s, when the country was undergoing a major boom in baseload plant construction. Prior to 2021, the highest requested annual rate increase total occurred in 1981, when utilities put forth petitions for about $13.1 billion in rate increases. Adjusting for inflation, however, the rate requests in the early 1980s still outpace current rate requests.

Comments on RRA's methodology

RRA endeavors to follow all "major" rate cases for investor-owned utilities nationwide, with major defined as a case in which the utility's request would result in a rate change of at least $5 million or in which the commission approves a rate change of at least $3 million. In addition to base rate cases, the rate case history database includes details regarding certain limited-issue rider proceedings, primarily those that involve significant rate base additions that are recognized outside of a general rate case. In some of these cases, the rate change coverage criteria may not apply.

Moreover, some US utilities operate under formula-based rate plans where rates are adjusted annually (or on some other periodic basis) without a full rate case filing and generally without revisiting the authorized return. These proceedings are generally not included in the database and, therefore, would not be included in the tally of cases or the aggregate rate change stats. Details are discussed in the footnotes to the case in which the formula rate plan was established.
